stem: A finite field \( F \) has characteristic \( [[p]] \), and as a vector space over its prime subfield \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) it has dimension \( [[k]] \). How many elements does \( F \) have?
solution: A field of characteristic \( p \) that is a \( k \)-dimensional vector space over its prime subfield \( \mathbb{Z}_{p} \) has \( p^{k} \) elements (one coordinate vector per element). So \( |F| = [[p]]^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( p \cdot k \) instead of raising \( p \) to the power \( k \).
This swaps base and exponent, using \( k^{p} \) instead of \( p^{k} \).
This is \( p^{k} - 1 \), the order of the multiplicative group \( F^{\times} \), not of the whole field.
This uses dimension \( k-1 \); the field is \( k \)-dimensional over its prime subfield.
This is one more than the correct number of elements.
This is two more than the correct number of elements.

stem: A finite field \( F \) has exactly \( [[qval]] \) elements. Since the order of a finite field must be a prime power, \( [[qval]] \) can be written uniquely as \( q^{m} \) for some prime \( q \) and positive integer \( m \). What is the prime \( q \), i.e. the characteristic of \( F \)?
solution: Writing \( [[qval]] \) in prime-power form gives \( [[qval]] = [[p]]^{[[k]]} \), so the prime base (the characteristic of \( F \)) is \( q = [[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ORDER of the field \( [[qval]] \) itself, not the prime base of its prime-power factorization.
This is the exponent \( [[k]] \) in \( [[qval]] = p^{[[k]]} \), not the prime base.
This subtracts one from the field's order, unrelated to extracting the prime base.
This multiplies the prime by the exponent instead of reporting the prime alone.
This is one more than the correct characteristic.
This is one fewer than the correct characteristic.

stem: A finite field \( F \) has characteristic \( [[p]] \) and exactly \( [[qval]] \) elements. Since \( |F| \) must equal \( [[p]]^{m} \) for some positive integer \( m \), what is \( m \) (the dimension of \( F \) as a vector space over its prime subfield \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \))?
distractor_rationales: This is the field's full order \( [[qval]] \), not the exponent \( m \) in its prime-power factorization.
This is the characteristic \( [[p]] \) itself, not the exponent \( m \).
This divides the order by the characteristic once, giving \( p^{[[k]]-1} \) rather than isolating the exponent \( [[k]] \).
This is one more than the correct dimension.
This is one fewer than the correct dimension.
This multiplies the exponent by the characteristic instead of reporting the exponent alone.

stem: A finite field has characteristic \( [[p]] \) and is a \( [[k]] \)-dimensional vector space over its prime subfield \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). Which of the following could be the number of elements of this field? (Recall: a finite field's order must be a power of a single prime.)
solution: \( [[qval]] = [[p]]^{[[k]]} \) is a power of the single prime \( [[p]] \), so it is a valid finite field order. Every other listed number has at least two DISTINCT prime factors (e.g. it is divisible by both \( [[r]] \) and/or \( [[s]] \) together with \( [[p]] \)), so it cannot be the order of any finite field.
distractor_rationales: This has two distinct prime factors, \( [[p]] \) and \( [[r]] \), so it is not a prime power.
This has two distinct prime factors, \( [[p]] \) and \( [[s]] \), so it is not a prime power.
This has two distinct prime factors, \( [[r]] \) and \( [[s]] \), so it is not a prime power.
This has three distinct prime factors, \( [[p]] \), \( [[r]] \), and \( [[s]] \), so it is not a prime power.
This has two distinct prime factors, \( [[r]] \) and \( [[s]] \), so it is not a prime power.
This has two distinct prime factors, \( [[s]] \) and \( [[r]] \), so it is not a prime power.
